one_line_takeaway: A small, model-agnostic segment-and-classify preprocessor (SaT + fine-tuned BERT, ~387M params) that strips GCG/AmpleGCG-style adversarial suffixes and drops jailbreak ASR from ~80-93% to under 4% on well-aligned models with negligible benign-task loss -- though a poorly aligned target (GPT3.5) still leaks at ~17% and it only defends the suffix-appending threat.
key_finding: ASF sharply reduces attack success rate (ASR -> ASR') while barely affecting benign-task performance. The segment classifier reaches F1 98.5% (validation) / 98.4% (held-out test). On the end-to-end defense (Table 1a): Llama-2-7b-chat 81.1% -> 1.8%; Llama-2-7b-chat with AmpleGCG-plus suffixes 93.1% -> 4.0%; GPT4-0613(+) 18.4% -> 3.9%. Against the AIR/AID techniques that bypass perplexity defenses (Table 1b, 50-sample subset): GPT3.5 AIR 80% -> 18%, AID 78% -> 16%; GPT4(+) AIR 2% -> 0%, AID 2% -> 0%. Across sanitizations, 76.4% of suffixes were completely removed leaving the original prompt intact, while 2.9% of returned prompts were empty (over-removal). Non-adversarial degradation is minimal (Table 2): worst observed drop is WinoGrande on Llama-3.1-8B at -5.1%; most shifts are within +/-1% and within generation stochasticity. Total defense footprint is ~387M parameters (SaT 12l-SM + bert-base-uncased) needing ~1.7GB extra GPU memory, adding no forward passes or token cost to the protected LLM.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Adversarial Suffix Filtering (ASF), a lightweight, model-agnostic input-preprocessing pipeline that detects and removes adversarial suffixes from LLM prompts before they reach the target model. ASF's own novelty is the segment-level detection design: it splits an incoming prompt into sentence-like segments with an off-the-shelf Segment-any-Text (SaT) model, runs each segment through a BERT classifier the authors fine-tune specifically to flag adversarial-suffix content, then applies post-processing heuristics before deleting flagged segments (or raising an exception in 'warn' mode). The key insight the authors claim as their own is that because prior work (Liao and Sun's AmpleGCG) showed an LLM can be trained to GENERATE effective adversarial suffixes, those suffixes must follow a learnable, detectable pattern and are therefore classifiable by a small discriminative model. This is the paper's contribution, distinct from the cited defenses (SmoothLLM, perplexity filters, erase-and-check, Llama Guard) that it surveys in Related Work but does not adopt.
threat_model: The paper states its threat model explicitly (Section 1 and Section 2): an adversary can only APPEND a malicious suffix s to a user's plaintext query x; the adversary has no control over the model's parameters or system-level directives and no interaction with the model beyond issuing plaintext queries. The user's original prompt is presumed benign, and the suffix (often gibberish or out-of-distribution tokens, e.g. GCG-style) is designed solely to jailbreak the aligned model's safety guardrails. The defense's goal is to identify and strip the appended suffix s before inference. ASF assumes the underlying target LLM is already safety-aligned; it only removes suffixes and makes no judgment about whether the residual prompt is itself benign or malicious.
limitations: The paper's own reported limitations and weak spots: (1) The GPT3.5-0125 result is the clear failure case: standard-suffix ASR only drops 92.1% -> 16.9%, and even AIR/AID only fall to 18%/16% -- the authors attribute this to GPT3.5 being poorly aligned (2.7% benign ASR), and show an example where a natural-looking 'involves grammar' fragment jailbreaks it and survives filtering. (2) Reliance on accurate prompt/suffix segmentation; when SaT fails to isolate the suffix, ASF can over-remove benign text (2.9% empty outputs). (3) False positives from an imperfect BERT classifier, mitigated only by hand-tuned keyword heuristics whose very necessity signals per-deployment calibration is required; ASF deliberately errs toward aggressive removal, sacrificing specificity. (4) Scope is limited to suffix-style attacks -- prefix, interleaved, mixed-context, hand-crafted, or multilingual injections are out of scope and could evade it. (5) Effectiveness is bounded by the assumption that the underlying model is aligned; ASF only strips suffixes and does not filter an intrinsically harmful base prompt. Methodological caveats: no error bars are reported (paper argues large effect sizes make this moot), Vicuna was excluded because it self-jailbreaks in 53.5% of cases, k was capped at 20 beams due to memory, and code/checkpoints were not released at submission time.

datasets_used: AmpleGCG suffix dataset (Liao and Sun) merged and de-duplicated to 419,429 unique adversarial suffixes generated via GCG, AmpleGCG, and AmpleGCG-plus
Stanford Alpaca instruction dataset (52,000 benign instructions) as benign prompt source
MaliciousInstruct (100 harmful queries across 10 categories) -- held out for evaluation
AdvBench (520 harmful instructions) -- held out for evaluation
LM Evaluation Harness tasks for benign utility: TruthfulQA-gen, GSM8k, ARC-challenge, HellaSwag, WinoGrande
quantitative_results: Segment classifier F1: 98.5% validation, 98.4% held-out test
AIR on GPT3.5: 80.0% -> 18.0%; AID on GPT3.5: 78.0% -> 16.0% (50-sample subset)
76.4% of suffixes fully removed; 2.9% of sanitized prompts returned empty (over-removal)
Non-adversarial worst-case degradation: WinoGrande Llama-3.1-8B -5.1%, HellaSwag Llama-8B -3.4%, HellaSwag Mistral-7B -3.0%; generate_until tasks within +/-1.5%
Vicuna excluded: 53.5% benign ASR (jailbroken without any suffix); GPT3.5-0125 benign ASR 2.7%
Defense footprint: ~387M parameters (SaT 12l-SM + bert-base-uncased), ~1.7GB additional GPU memory, single-pass, no added forward passes/tokens for target LLM
Training: AdamW lr 5e-5, 3 epochs with early stopping; 70/15/15 split; k=20 attack beams; compute 3xRTX8000 + 1xA10 over ~3 weeks
baselines_compared: The paper's primary empirical comparison is each model's ASR before vs after ASF (ASR vs ASR'); it does NOT run head-to-head experiments against other defense systems

Perplexity-based defenses (Alon & Kamfonas; Jain et al.) are compared implicitly: ASF is tested against AIR/AID suffixes specifically engineered to bypass those PPL filters, and succeeds where PPL defenses would fail (0% detection)
Attack models used as the adversary: GCG (Zou et al.), AmpleGCG and AmpleGCG-plus (two variants each); HarmBench-cls used as the jailbreak judge

Qualitatively contrasted (Related Work only, not benchmarked): adversarial training/fine-tuning, StruQ structured queries, SmoothLLM random perturbation, Bergeron conscience model, Llama Guard, and Kumar et al. erase-and-check certified defense
